content: As the Earth rotates, it also orbits around the sun in an elliptical path. This journey takes approximately 365 days to complete. The key to understanding seasons lies in the Earth's tilt, about 23.5 degrees, relative to its orbital plane around the sun. This tilt means that, throughout the year, different parts of the Earth receive varying amounts of sunlight. When the part of the Earth where you are is tilted towards the sun, you receive more sunlight and experience longer days, resulting in warmer temperatures and summer. Conversely, when your part of the Earth is tilted away from the sun, you receive less sunlight, leading to colder temperatures and winter. The periods in between, when the Earth is not significantly tilted towards or away from the sun, are spring and autumn, characterized by milder temperatures. This perpetual tilt and orbit combination creates the seasonal changes we observe.
